nodes.title: Fault Tolerance in Distributed Systems Chapter 8
Saeid Sheikhi
nodes.content:
PhD candidate in Computer Science and Engineering Future Computing Group (FCG) University of Oulu

February 11, 2026

nodes.title: Part I: Fundamentals
nodes.content:
- 1 Introduction to Fault Tolerance
- 2 Failure Models
- 3 Redundancy & Process Groups
nodes.summary: - 1 Introduction to Fault Tolerance - 2 Failure Models - 3 Redundancy & Process Groups

nodes.title: Part II: Consensus
nodes.content:
- 4 Consensus with Crash Failures
- 5 Raft & Paxos Algorithms
- 6 Byzantine Fault Tolerance
nodes.summary: - 4 Consensus with Crash Failures - 5 Raft & Paxos Algorithms - 6 Byzantine Fault Tolerance

nodes.title: Part III: Communication
nodes.content:
- 7 Reliable Client-Server Communication
- 8 Reliable Group Communication
- 9 Atomic Multicast
nodes.summary: - 7 Reliable Client-Server Communication - 8 Reliable Group Communication - 9 Atomic Multicast

nodes.title: Part IV: Recovery
nodes.content:
- 10 Distributed Commit (2PC)
- 11 Checkpointing & Recovery

nodes.summary: - 10 Distributed Commit (2PC) - 11 Checkpointing & Recovery - 12 The CAP Theorem

nodes.title: What Makes Distributed Systems Different?
nodes.content:
- Part of system fails while rest continues
- Remaining part operates 'seemingly correctly'
- System must automatically recover
nodes.summary: - Part of system fails while rest continues - Remaining part operates 'seemingly correctly' - System must automatically recover

nodes.title: Goal of Fault Tolerance:
nodes.content:
- Continue operating during repairs
- Mask failures from users
- Provide dependable services
- Key Insight: A distributed system is expected to be fault tolerant - failures are the norm, not the exception
nodes.summary: - Continue operating during repairs - Mask failures from users - Provide dependable services - Key Insight: A distributed system is expected to be fault tolerant - failures are the norm, not the exception

nodes.title: Reliability
nodes.content:
System runs continuously without failure

- Defined over a time interval
- Different from availability!
nodes.summary: System runs continuously without failure - Defined over a time interval - Different from availability!

nodes.title: Safety
nodes.content:
No catastrophic event when system fails

- Critical for control systems
- Nuclear plants, space systems
nodes.summary: No catastrophic event when system fails - Critical for control systems - Nuclear plants, space systems

nodes.title: High Availability, Low Reliability
nodes.content:
System goes down for 1 random millisecond every hour

- Availability: > 99.9999%
- But still unreliable !
- Unpredictable failures

The two metrics are NOT the same, design for both!
nodes.summary: System goes down for 1 random millisecond every hour - Availability: > 99.9999% - But still unreliable ! - Unpredictable failures The two metrics are NOT the same, design for both!

nodes.title: Example:
nodes.content:
- Fault: Programming bug
- Error: Bad code branch executed
- Failure: Program crashes
nodes.summary: - Fault: Programming bug - Error: Bad code branch executed - Failure: Program crashes

nodes.title: Types of Faults
nodes.content:
| Type | Icon | Description | Example |
|----------------------|------------------------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Transient | Occurs once, then disap- pears | Bird | through mi- crowave beam |
| Intermittent | Appears, vanishes, pears | reap- | Loose connector |
| Permanent | Continues until component replaced | | Burnt chip, software bug |

Transient Easiest to handle (retry works)

Intermittent Hardest to diagnose ('works when tested')

Permanent Requires replacement (clear failure)
nodes.summary: | Type | Icon | Description | Example | |----------------------|------------------------------------|---------------|--------------------------| | Transient | Occurs once, then disap- pears | Bird | through mi- crowave b...

nodes.title: Failure Models - Taxonomy
nodes.content:
| Type | Description | Severity |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Crash failure Omission failure Receive omission Send omission Timing failure Response failure Value failure State-transition Arbitrary (Byzantine) | Halts, but correct until halt Fails to respond to requests Fails to receive messages Fails to send messages Response outside time interval Response is incorrect Wrong value returned Wrong flow of control Any response, any time | ○ Low ○ Low ○ ○ ○ Medium ○ Medium ○ ○ ○ High |

nodes.title: What Makes Byzantine Failures Dangerous?
nodes.content:
- Server produces output it should never have
- Output cannot be detected as incorrect
- Named after Byzantine Empire intrigues
nodes.summary: - Server produces output it should never have - Output cannot be detected as incorrect - Named after Byzantine Empire intrigues

nodes.title: Key Challenge:
nodes.content:
- Could be malicious OR buggy software
- Cannot distinguish benign from malicious
- Must assume the worst

Same faulty server sends different values to different receivers!
nodes.summary: - Could be malicious OR buggy software - Cannot distinguish benign from malicious - Must assume the worst Same faulty server sends different values to different receivers!

nodes.title: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Systems
nodes.content:
| System | Characteristics | Failure Detection |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Synchronous | Bounded process speeds & message delivery times | Can conclude crash if no re- sponse when expected |
| Asynchronous | No timing assumptions | Cannot conclude crash - may just be slow |
| Partially Synchronous | Usually synchronous, sometimes not | Most realistic model |

'Most of the time it behaves as a synchronous system, yet there is no bound on the time that it behaves asynchronously.'

nodes.title: Information Redundancy
nodes.content:
Extra bits for recovery

- Hamming codes
- Error-correcting codes
- Checksums
nodes.summary: Extra bits for recovery - Hamming codes - Error-correcting codes - Checksums

nodes.title: Physical Redundancy
nodes.content:
Extra equipment/processes

- Replicated servers
- Backup systems
- Process groups
nodes.summary: Extra equipment/processes - Replicated servers - Backup systems - Process groups

nodes.title: Why It Works:
nodes.content:
- If A2 fails: V1, V2, V3 get 2 good + 1 bad
- Each voter outputs correct value
- Failure is completely masked
nodes.summary: - If A2 fails: V1, V2, V3 get 2 good + 1 bad - Each voter outputs correct value - Failure is completely masked

nodes.title: How TMR Works:
nodes.content:
- Each device replicated 3 times
- Voters after each stage
- 3 inputs → 1 output
- Majority vote wins
- Key Benefit: A faulty voter is no different from a faulty component, both are voted down by subsequent stages
nodes.summary: - Each device replicated 3 times - Voters after each stage - 3 inputs → 1 output - Majority vote wins - Key Benefit: A faulty voter is no different from a faulty component, both are voted down by subsequent stages

nodes.title: Definition
nodes.content: 'When a message is sent to the group itself, all members of the group receive it .'
nodes.summary: 'When a message is sent to the group itself, all members of the group receive it .'

nodes.title: Purpose:
nodes.content:
- Process P sends to group Q = { Q 1 , ., Q N }
- P doesn't need to know:
- Who they are
- How many there are
- Where they are located
- Group Q appears as single logical process
nodes.summary: - Process P sends to group Q = { Q 1 , ., Q N } - P doesn't need to know: - Who they are - How many there are - Where they are located - Group Q appears as single logical process

nodes.title: Hierarchical Group
nodes.content:
- One coordinator + workers
- + Fast decision making
- + Less communication
- -Single point of failure
- -If coordinator crashes, group halts
nodes.summary: - One coordinator + workers - + Fast decision making - + Less communication - -Single point of failure - -If coordinator crashes, group halts

nodes.title: Flat Group
nodes.content:
- All processes equal
- + No single point of failure
- + If one crashes, group continues
- -Decisions require voting
- -More overhead
nodes.summary: - All processes equal - + No single point of failure - + If one crashes, group continues - -Decisions require voting - -More overhead

nodes.title: For k-fault tolerance:
nodes.content:
| Failure Type | Processes Needed | Reason |
|-----------------------|--------------------|------------------------------|
| Silent (crash) | k + 1 | If k stop, one still answers |
| Arbitrary (Byzantine) | 2k + 1 | Need k+1 correct majority |

Byzantine Failures

k

= 2 ⇒ 5 servers
nodes.summary: | Failure Type | Processes Needed | Reason | |-----------------------|--------------------|------------------------------| | Silent (crash) | k + 1 | If k stop, one still answers | | Arbitrary (Byzantine) | 2k + 1 | Need...

nodes.title: Fundamental Requirement
nodes.content: 'In a fault-tolerant process group, each nonfaulty process executes the same commands , in the same order , as every other nonfaulty process.'
nodes.summary: 'In a fault-tolerant process group, each nonfaulty process executes the same commands , in the same order , as every other nonfaulty process.'

nodes.title: Why Consensus is Hard:
nodes.content:
- Without failures → easy
- With failures → tricky!
- Cannot distinguish slow from crashed
- Messages may be lost

All must agree on operation order!
nodes.summary: - Without failures → easy - With failures → tricky! - Cannot distinguish slow from crashed - Messages may be lost All must agree on operation order!

nodes.title: Algorithm (assuming fail-stop):
nodes.content:
- 1 Each process maintains list of proposed commands
- 2 In each round: send list to all other processes
- 3 End of round: merge all received lists
- 4 Deterministically select command to execute
- Key Insight: If all have same list → all select same command (Selection algorithm must be identical for all processes)
nodes.summary: - 1 Each process maintains list of proposed commands - 2 In each round: send list to all other processes - 3 End of round: merge all received lists - 4 Deterministically select command to execute - Key Insight: If all ha...

nodes.title: Why Raft?
nodes.content:
- Reaction to Paxos complexity
- Designed for understandability
- Primary-backup protocol
nodes.summary: - Reaction to Paxos complexity - Designed for understandability - Primary-backup protocol

nodes.title: Key Components:
nodes.content:
- Group of ∼ 5 replicated servers
- Each maintains a log of operations
- One server is leader
- Leader decides operation order

Leader's log = collective state of server group

Log Replication
nodes.summary: - Group of ∼ 5 replicated servers - Each maintains a log of operations - One server is leader - Leader decides operation order Leader's log = collective state of server group Log Replication

nodes.title: Step-by-Step:
nodes.content:
- 1 Client sends request to leader
- 2 Leader appends ⟨ op, term, index ⟩ to log
- 3 Leader sends log entry to followers
- 4 Followers copy, send ACK
- 5 Majority ACK → leader commits
- 6 Leader responds to client

Commit condition: Majority acknowledgment ensures consensus
nodes.summary: - 1 Client sends request to leader - 2 Leader appends ⟨ op, term, index ⟩ to log - 3 Leader sends log entry to followers - 4 Followers copy, send ACK - 5 Majority ACK → leader commits - 6 Leader responds to client Commit...

nodes.title: Edge Case:
nodes.content:
- Leader crashed after executing
- But before informing followers
- Solution: New leader handles old operation when processing new one
nodes.summary: - Leader crashed after executing - But before informing followers - Solution: New leader handles old operation when processing new one

nodes.title: When Leader Crashes:
nodes.content:
- 1 New leader elected
- 2 New leader's log becomes state
- 3 Server won't vote for candidate with less up-to-date log

New leader's log is at least subset of majority's logs (otherwise wouldn't be elected)
nodes.summary: - 1 New leader elected - 2 New leader's log becomes state - 3 Server won't vote for candidate with less up-to-date log New leader's log is at least subset of majority's logs (otherwise wouldn't be elected)

nodes.title: Historical Note:
nodes.content:
- Published 1989 by Leslie Lamport
- Took ∼ decade to be recognized
- 'Difficult to explain and understand'
- Implementations often differ
nodes.summary: - Published 1989 by Leslie Lamport - Took ∼ decade to be recognized - 'Difficult to explain and understand' - Implementations often differ

nodes.title: Assumptions:
nodes.content:
- Partially synchronous system
- Messages may be lost, duplicated, reordered
- Corrupted messages detectable
- Crash failures only (not Byzantine)
nodes.summary: - Partially synchronous system - Messages may be lost, duplicated, reordered - Corrupted messages detectable - Crash failures only (not Byzantine)

nodes.title: 1a (Prepare):
nodes.content:
- Proposer selects number m
- Creates timestamp t = (m, i)
- Sends prepare(t) to acceptors
nodes.summary: - Proposer selects number m - Creates timestamp t = (m, i) - Sends prepare(t) to acceptors

nodes.title: 1b (Promise):
nodes.content:
- If t is highest: return promise(t)
- If proposal accepted: return it too
- Otherwise: do nothing
nodes.summary: - If t is highest: return promise(t) - If proposal accepted: return it too - Otherwise: do nothing

nodes.title: 2a (Accept):
nodes.content:
- No accepted op received: send own
- Otherwise: adopt highest timestamp op
nodes.summary: - No accepted op received: send own - Otherwise: adopt highest timestamp op

nodes.title: 2b (Learn):
nodes.content:
- Acceptor accepts if no higher promise
- Sends learn(o) to learners
- Learner executes when majority confirms

Key: Multiple proposals may be accepted, but all with same operation
nodes.summary: - Acceptor accepts if no higher promise - Sends learn(o) to learners - Learner executes when majority confirms Key: Multiple proposals may be accepted, but all with same operation

nodes.title: With 3 processes (k=1):
nodes.summary: - Faulty primary sends T to B1, F to B2 - Each backup receives { T, F } - Cannot decide!

nodes.title: Or faulty backup forwards wrong value:
nodes.content:
- Same result
- Cannot distinguish correct from incorrect

Conclusion: 3 processes insufficient for 1 Byzantine failure
nodes.summary: - Same result - Cannot distinguish correct from incorrect Conclusion: 3 processes insufficient for 1 Byzantine failure

nodes.title: Requirements:
nodes.content:
- BA1: Every nonfaulty backup stores same value
- BA2: If primary nonfaulty, backups store what primary sent
nodes.summary: - BA1: Every nonfaulty backup stores same value - BA2: If primary nonfaulty, backups store what primary sent

nodes.title: With 4 processes (k=1):
nodes.content:
- Even if primary sends inconsistent values
- After forwarding: each has { T, T, F }
- Majority vote works!

All agree on T (majority)
nodes.summary: - Even if primary sends inconsistent values - After forwarding: each has { T, T, F } - Majority vote works! All agree on T (majority)

nodes.title: Key Achievement:
nodes.content:
- Combines safety, liveness, performance
- Uses 3k + 1 replica servers
- Developed by Castro & Liskov (2002)
nodes.summary: - Combines safety, liveness, performance - Uses 3k + 1 replica servers - Developed by Castro & Liskov (2002)

nodes.title: Assumptions:
nodes.content:
- No assumptions about faulty behavior
- Messages may be lost, delayed, reordered
- Message sender identifiable (signatures)
nodes.summary: - No assumptions about faulty behavior - Messages may be lost, delayed, reordered - Message sender identifiable (signatures)
